question_text: A student measures the weight of a 2 kg object using a newtonmeter on Earth (g = 10 N/kg). Which of the following best explains why the object's weight would be different on Jupiter, where g ≈ 25 N/kg?

options.label: A
options.text: The object's mass increases on Jupiter because gravity is stronger.

options.label: B
options.text: Jupiter has a much stronger gravitational field, so the gravitational force on the object is greater, making its weight larger.

options.label: C
options.text: The newtonmeter is broken on Jupiter and gives false readings.

options.label: D
options.text: The object shrinks on Jupiter, so it weighs less even though gravity is stronger.

explanation: Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object and depends on both mass and gravitational field strength (W = m × g). Jupiter's stronger gravitational field (25 N/kg vs 10 N/kg on Earth) means the same 2 kg object experiences a greater downward force. The object's mass doesn't change, but the gravitational pull does, so the weight increases to 50 N on Jupiter compared to 20 N on Earth.
